title: Multi-Label Metrics
concept: Evaluating multi-label models requires metrics like Precision, Recall, and Jaccard Similarity to assess sets of labels.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the effect of lowering the classification threshold for a specific class?

options.text: It increases positive predictions, raising recall and lowering precision.
It increases precision and lowers recall.
It has no effect on final predictions.

exercises.explain: Correct! Lowering the threshold makes it easier to predict positive cases, catching more actual items (higher recall).

cards.title: Getting the order right
cards.body: If you ask a shopper to buy milk, bread, and eggs, and they come back with milk, bread, and a box of cookies, how do you grade them? They got 2 out of 3 right, but also added an extra item. Multi-label evaluation solves this grading puzzle.

cards.title: Multi-label Evaluation
cards.body: Evaluating multi-label models goes beyond simple accuracy. We use metrics like Precision (of predicted tags, how many are correct), Recall (of actual tags, how many did we find), and the Jaccard Index (Intersection over Union of tag sets).

cards.title: A search engine query
cards.body: Think of a search engine. Precision is like checking if the first page of results contains only relevant links. Recall is like checking if the search engine found all relevant web pages on the entire internet.

cards.title: Jaccard similarity example
cards.body: If actual tags are {cat, dog, tree} and model predicts {cat, dog, car}, the intersection is {cat, dog} (size 2). The union is {cat, dog, tree, car} (size 4). The Jaccard Index is 2 / 4 = 0.50.

cards.title: Accuracy can be too strict
cards.body: You might think standard accuracy (checking if the predicted set matches the target set exactly) is a good metric. However, if a model predicts 9 out of 10 tags correctly, exact-match accuracy scores it as 0% correct! We need 'subset accuracy' instead.

cards.title: Set Overlap (Jaccard)
cards.body: How the Jaccard Index measures set similarity:

right.text:
All unique labels: {cat, dog, tree, car}
Count: 4
Denominator of Jaccard

exercises.prompt: If a model predicts labels {Apple, Banana} for an image whose actual labels are {Apple, Orange}, what is the Jaccard Index (IoU)?
exercises.explain: The intersection is {Apple} (size 1). The union is {Apple, Banana, Orange} (size 3). Jaccard is 1/3 ≈ 0.33.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Exact-match accuracy requires every single predicted label to match the target labels exactly for a sample to be considered correct.
exercises.explain: True. Exact-match accuracy (or subset accuracy) is very strict, counting a prediction as correct only if the set matches exactly.
Jaccard similarity measures the ratio of shared elements to the total set of unique elements.
exercises.sentence: The Jaccard Index is computed as the intersection over the ___ of two label sets.

exercises.prompt: Which metric evaluates how complete the model's predictions are (i.e. if it missed any true labels)?
exercises.explain: Recall measures the percentage of actual positive labels that the model successfully retrieved.

exercises.prompt: A model tags medical images. It predicts {fracture, bleeding} for a scan that only has {fracture}. What are the Precision and Recall values for this prediction?

options.text: Precision = 0.50 (1 of 2 predictions correct); Recall = 1.00 (found the 1 true label).
Both values are 0.00 because the sets did not match exactly.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The model found all the true labels (Recall = 1.0), but half of its predictions were incorrect (Precision = 0.50). This detailed analysis is vital for real-world tasks. Fantastic job completing the Multi-Label Classification unit!
